name: Bèrnia Loop Trail
name_original: La Serra de Bèrnia (PR-CV 7) — Running circular
description: Jogging the difficult 5.5-mile La Serra de Bèrnia (PR-CV 7) circular route offers panoramic views and a unique tunnel passage.
region_intro: Jogging routes Xaló traverse the diverse landscapes of the Pop Valley, characterized by rolling hills and extensive vineyards. The region features routes along the River Gorgos and more challenging trails within the Sierra de Bèrnia mountain range. This area offers varied terrain, from agricultural paths to mountain and forest trails, suitable for different running preferences. Best jogging routes around Xaló The most popular jogging route is Bèrnia Loop Trail, a 5.5 miles (8.9 km) trail that takes 1 hour 21 minutes to complete. This difficult route offers panoramic views within the Sierra de Bèrnia. Another top favourite among local runners is View of the Jalón Valley – Cruz de Caminos Al Vent loop from Xaló, a difficult 8.9 miles (14.4 km) path. This route provides expansive views of the Jalón Valley. Local runners also love the El Penyot – Murtes Dam loop from Xaló, a 12.7 miles (20.4 km) trail leading through varied terrain, often completed in about 3 hours 22 minutes. Jogging around Xaló is defined by rolling hills, vineyards, and mountain trails, including those in the Sierra de Bèrnia. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate paths to more difficult ascents. The routes in Xaló are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from 10 reviews. More than 400 runners have used komoot to explore Xaló's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available around Xaló?
A: There are over 20 dedicated jogging routes around Xaló, offering a diverse range of experiences from easy vineyard paths to challenging mountain trails. The komoot community has explored these routes extensively, with over 400 runners using them.

Q: What is the best time of year for jogging in Xaló?
A: Xaló benefits from a Mediterranean climate, making spring and autumn ideal for running. During these seasons, you'll find mild temperatures perfect for exploring the Pop Valley's vineyards and the Sierra de Bèrnia's trails. While summers are warm, early mornings or late evenings are best to avoid the heat.

Q: Are there any family-friendly running trails in the Xaló area?
A: Yes, Xaló offers some easier routes suitable for families. While many trails are moderate to difficult, you can find paths that wind through the valley's agricultural landscapes, offering a gentler experience. Look for routes with less elevation gain for a more relaxed family run.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the jogging routes in Xaló?
A: Many of the trails around Xaló are suitable for running with dogs, especially those through the vineyards and along the River Gorgos. However, always ensure your dog is on a leash, and be mindful of local regulations, especially in protected areas or near livestock. Remember to carry water for both yourself and your pet.

Q: Are there circular running routes available in Xaló?
A: Yes, many of the running routes in Xaló are circular, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. For example, the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40379083" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Jalón Valley – Cruz de Caminos Al Vent loop from Xaló&lt;/a&gt; offers expansive views, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10356667" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Velosol Cycling Cafe – Peacocks in Rural Spain loop from Xaló&lt;/a&gt; provides a scenic run through rural landscapes.

Q: What kind of natural attractions or landmarks can I see while running in Xaló?
A: Xaló's running routes offer a wealth of natural beauty and historical landmarks. You can explore the lush vineyards of the Pop Valley, run along the River Gorgos, or venture into the dramatic Sierra de Bèrnia. Notable attractions include the ruins of Aixa Castle, offering panoramic views, and the unique Forat de Bèrnia natural tunnel. You might also encounter the 18th-century Sapena Pool (Bassa Els Arcs Pool) with its distinctive columns and arches. For specific natural features, consider routes that pass by the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/398248" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Algar Springs Waterfalls&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/964080" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gorgos Riverbed and Aqueduct in Gata de Gorgos&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Where can I park when accessing running trails in Xaló?
A: Parking is generally available in Xaló town itself, from where many routes begin or are easily accessible. For trails leading into the Sierra de Bèrnia, specific parking areas might be available closer to the trailheads, though these can be limited. It's advisable to check specific route details for recommended parking spots.

Q: Are there public transport options to reach the running routes from Xaló?
A: While Xaló is well-connected within the Pop Valley, public transport options directly to specific trailheads, especially in more remote mountain areas like the Sierra de Bèrnia, can be limited. Most runners typically start their routes from Xaló town or drive to a convenient starting point. Local bus services connect Xaló with nearby villages, which might offer access to some trails.

Q: Are there any easy running routes for beginners in Xaló?
A: Yes, Xaló offers a couple of easy running routes perfect for beginners or those looking for a less strenuous run. These routes typically traverse the flatter sections of the Pop Valley, winding through vineyards and agricultural landscapes. They provide a gentle introduction to the region's beauty without significant elevation changes.

Q: What do other runners enjoy most about jogging in Xaló?
A: The running routes in Xaló are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars. Reviewers often praise the stunning and varied landscapes, from the peaceful vineyards of the Pop Valley to the dramatic mountain views of the Sierra de Bèrnia. The extensive network of trails and the opportunity to discover charming villages and historical sites are also frequently highlighted.

Q: Are there any challenging running routes for experienced runners?
A: Absolutely. Xaló is a fantastic destination for experienced runners seeking a challenge. The region boasts 12 difficult routes, many of which ascend into the Sierra de Bèrnia. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e872579891/bernia-loop-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bèrnia Loop Trail&lt;/a&gt;, for instance, is a difficult 5.5-mile route with significant elevation gain, offering panoramic views and the unique experience of passing through the Forat de Bèrnia natural tunnel. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31520910" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Penyot – Murtes Dam loop from Xaló&lt;/a&gt;, a 12.7-mile trail with considerable ascent.

Q: Can I find cafes or places to eat near the running trails in Xaló?
A: Yes, Xaló and the surrounding villages in the Vall de Pop offer various cafes, restaurants, and wineries where you can refuel after your run. Many routes pass through or near these charming towns, allowing you to enjoy local cuisine or a refreshing drink. The area is also known for its wine production, so you might find routes that lead past local bodegas.
tips:
The Fort of Bèrnia, built in 1562 by the royal engineer Juan Bautista Antoneli, was a clear and magnificent exponent of the Renaissance military architecture of the Ottomans on the coast. In 1612, and given its ineffectiveness due to its distance from the coast, King Philip III ordered its demolition.

Of the Fort you can still see the arches, the moat, the bastion, the cistern and the remains of the walls. Its extension was 10 meters long by 110 meters wide. It is declared B.I.C. (Asset of Cultural Interest) since 1997.

The explanatory panels show the former functioning and use of the Fort.

Through a small advance you can go from the south side of the mountain range to the north side. The advance should be crossed about 80 meters in a low inclined position. The walls of the tunnel are roughly carved.

faqs.question: Is La Serra de Bèrnia (PR-CV 7) — Running circular suitable for all runners?
faqs.answer: This trail is generally rated as having medium to high difficulty, making it a challenging run. It's best suited for experienced trail runners who are comfortable with technical, rocky terrain, steep ascents and descents, and significant elevation changes. Beginners or those looking for a smooth, continuous jogging path might find it too demanding.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is varied and technical. You'll encounter uneven and rocky paths, with sections requiring moderate climbing and even some easy scrambling, especially on the northern side leading to the Forat de Bèrnia. The southern side is rockier and more exposed, with loose rocks and narrow sections. Due to the limestone, it can be slippery, so sure-footedness and sturdy trail shoes are essential.

faqs.question: What notable features and viewpoints can I expect on the trail?
faqs.answer: The route offers breathtaking panoramic views and unique features. You'll pass through the distinctive Forat de Bèrnia, a natural tunnel cutting through the mountain. Further along, you can explore the ruins of the 16th-century Bèrnia Fort. The trail also provides diverse scenery, from the greener northern slopes to the sunnier southern face, with views extending to the bay of Altea, Serra Gelada Natural Park, and the Mediterranean Sea.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trailhead?
faqs.answer: The most common starting point for the PR-CV 7 is near the Font de Bèrnia or the Cases de Bèrnia. There are usually designated parking areas available in these vicinities. It's advisable to arrive early, especially on weekends or during peak season, as parking can fill up.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to run this trail?
faqs.answer: Spring and autumn are generally the best seasons for running this trail. The temperatures are milder, making the strenuous sections more manageable. Summers can be very hot, especially on the exposed southern side, so if you run then, start very early to avoid the midday sun. Winters can be pleasant, but be prepared for cooler temperatures and potentially slippery conditions if there has been recent rain.

faqs.question: Is this trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While dogs are generally allowed in the Sierras de Bèrnia y Ferrer Protected Landscape, the technical and rocky nature of this specific trail, including the narrow Forat de Bèrnia tunnel, might be challenging for some dogs. It's crucial to keep dogs on a leash to protect wildlife and for their safety on steep and exposed sections. Consider your dog's agility and comfort with rough terrain before bringing them.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for the Sierras de Bèrnia y Ferrer Protected Landscape?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to access the trails within the Sierras de Bèrnia y Ferrer Protected Landscape, including the PR-CV 7. However, always respect the natural environment and follow any local regulations or signage.

faqs.question: Given the trail's difficulty, are there any strategic tips for runners?
faqs.answer: Yes, for a more strategic approach, consider the direction you tackle the loop. Some runners prefer to ascend the steeper, more technical sections on the northern side first, getting the challenging climb to the Forat de Bèrnia out of the way. This leaves the descent on the southern side, which, while still rocky, might feel more manageable. Also, be prepared for the dramatic change in light and scenery when passing through the Forat tunnel.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this circular route, known as "Volta a Bèrnia," covers 100% of the official trail of the same name. It also intersects with smaller sections of other trails, such as "Camí del Fort," "[CIMA AT10] Bernía * A (recomendada)," and "Subida al Aspre."

faqs.question: Are there any facilities or water sources along the trail?
faqs.answer: There are no official facilities like restrooms or shops directly on the trail itself. While the route passes by the Font de Bèrnia, it's always best to carry sufficient water for the entire run, especially given the trail's duration and exposure. Plan to be self-sufficient regarding hydration and nutrition.

faqs.question: What essential gear should I bring for this run?
faqs.answer: Given the trail's technical nature and duration, essential gear includes sturdy trail running shoes with good grip, plenty of water (at least 1.5-2 liters), energy snacks, sun protection (hat, sunscreen, sunglasses), and a fully charged phone. A lightweight first-aid kit and a map or GPS device are also highly recommended due to the varied terrain and potential for isolated sections.
